summary: When deciding between going into management consulting or teaching after college, Lewis was torn between the two career paths. A Yale dean gave him a decision framework based on "option value": consulting preserves the option to teach later (the odds of moving from consulting into teaching stay high), whereas teaching forecloses the option to move into consulting later (those odds drop sharply). Facing a weak 2003 economy in the aftermath of the tech bubble, and unsure which path he actually wanted, Lewis used this asymmetry - not passion or interest - as the tiebreaker and chose consulting specifically because it kept both doors open.
quote: he basically said, look, let me teach you this thing called option value... if you go do management consulting right now, you can have, the odds of you being able to go be a teacher later are really high... If you go do a teaching right now, the odds of being able to go into management consulting later are really low. So, if you're not sure which one you want to do, pick the one that has more option value.

summary: Convoy's early speed wasn't a generic "move fast" startup platitude - it was a deliberate response to a specific, dated risk. In 2015 there was real market speculation that the truck/freight funding environment would dry up or change dramatically in 2016, so Lewis and his co-founders built an explicit work-back plan from company founding in April to closing a credible Series A within the same year - about eight months from starting the company. The urgency came from treating the funding window as closing, not from an assumption that speed itself is always the smart path.
quote: the market, back in 2015, there was a lot of speculation that the funding market would dry up or change dramatically in 2016... we created a work back plan that involved us getting to the point we could go credibly get a series A done in 2015 with basically eight months from finding the company.

summary: To make the Series A raise credible, Convoy set an explicit, minimal operating milestone rather than a vague growth goal: reach 100 shipments by a set date, even before they had a real product. The number wasn't chosen for its own sake - it was calibrated as the smallest volume that would let the team understand its own unit economics and tell a credible story to investors about scaling the business further.
quote: we're in trucking. We were new. Like, we need a hundred shipments, um, by this date. We didn't have a product. Like, we need to get a hundred shipments at least so we can understand our business enough to go have a really credible story about how to take it to the next level.

summary: Lewis asked Jeff Bezos (an Amazon investor of Convoy, and Lewis's former employer) how Amazon had arrived at its company values, expecting a top-down founding decision. Instead Bezos said Amazon deliberately did not set values at founding - they let the company operate and observe what behaviors were actually driving success, then formalized those into stated values only once the company reached roughly 90-100 employees. Convoy consciously copied this sequencing: rather than write values on day one, they waited until close to their one-year mark, looked back at what had actually made them successful, and wrote values from that evidence.
quote: we didn't just go set the values. We, the company developed and then we saw what was working, right? And then we thought about, okay, what's working, now let's capture those and encapsulate those values into a set of things that we actually live by... I think he said it was around like 90 or 100 people or something like that... we waited a little while, kind of towards about the year mark, we started working on our values.

summary: Lewis contrasts how goal-setting actually worked day-to-day at Amazon versus Microsoft. At Amazon, individual team goals (a 10- or 20-person team's goals) rolled all the way to the top without being aggregated into broader summary goals - meaning senior leadership, including the CEO, was directly tracking on the order of hundreds of individual team goals rather than a handful of high-level ones. At Microsoft, by contrast, goals were aggregated layer by layer until the CEO tracked only 15-20 rolled-up goals, which created a "watered-down" effect where an individual team member's own specific goal became invisible to leadership, reducing the felt accountability of hitting it.
quote: the goals that a lot of individual teams have, maybe a 10-person team or a 20-person team has, roll all the way up to the top. They don't get aggregated. So it's not like the CEO has 20 goals that he cares about. He's reading like 500 goals... And at Microsoft, for example... these seven goals were rolling to this goal. And then at that level, the other four goals were rolling to this goal. And then the CEO would have like 15 or 20 goals.

summary: Lewis uses Amazon's product-reviews team as a cautionary example of what happens when an organization conflates the problem it's solving with the solution it built. The original problem was customer confidence - how to give online shoppers the confidence to buy without a salesperson or the ability to touch the product. The solution was reviews, but once the team and all its hires were literally named and organized around "reviews," every future goal became about growing reviews (more reviews, more countries, more platforms, less fraud) rather than revisiting the underlying confidence problem. It took a deliberate return to the original problem to unlock later features like Q&A ("ask a question") and fit guidance ("does this run big or small") - features that had been sitting unbuilt because the team had structurally forgotten the problem in favor of optimizing its existing solution. Lewis explicitly imports this as an operating discipline at Convoy: when a team is fixated on improving a solution, someone should be able to ask "what was the problem?" and challenge whether the current solution still serves it.
quote: if you forget about the problem you're originally solving and you focus on improving the solution, you may actually miss like five other amazing opportunities... now you have things like ask a question on a product for Amazon to get an answer from another customer. That came out of this discussion... Those all happened in the last... few years. And that is something I'm taking to heart at Convoy.

summary: Building Convoy's MVP took about four months end-to-end - the engineering team was assembled in April/May and the product launched with real customers between the end of August and beginning of September of the same year. Lewis's dominant fear throughout that build wasn't a generic startup anxiety but a specific one: that after months of engineering work, the first real shippers would simply say they were fine with a truck they already had and didn't need the product, despite having told him during development that they needed it. That fear only resolved once Convoy's actual first customer, Guided Products (a Kent-based printed-materials company), completed its first real shipment on the platform.
quote: we spent four months building the product. We got the engineering team together April, May, and then built the MVP to launch with customers in the end of August and beginning of September. And until we actually had that first shipment, I just was like, man, what if nobody ever uses this?... everybody else lied to me along the way that they needed it.
